cards.back: The process where a function call is replaced with the code from the function body. This eliminates function call overhead but increases code size.

cards.text: To suggest that a function be inline-expanded, use the {{c1::inline}} keyword: {{c2::inline int max(int x, int y) { return (x > y) ? x : y; }}}

cards.front: Is the 'inline' keyword a command or a suggestion to the compiler?
cards.back: A suggestion. The compiler can ignore the inline keyword and is free to inline expand functions even without it. Modern compilers usually make better decisions than programmers.

cards.front: What are the trade-offs of inline expansion?
cards.back: Benefits: Eliminates function call overhead. Drawbacks: Increases executable size (code bloat), which can hurt performance due to less efficient caching.

cards.front: Why must inline functions be defined in every translation unit where they are used?
cards.back: Because the compiler needs to see the full definition to perform inline expansion. This means inline functions are typically defined in header files.

cards.front: Do inline functions violate the one-definition rule when defined in headers?
cards.back: No. Inline functions are exempt from the one-definition rule, allowing them to be defined in multiple translation units as long as all definitions are identical.

cards.text: An {{c1::inline variable}} (C++17) is a variable that is allowed to be defined in multiple translation units. Use the {{c2::inline}} keyword before the variable definition.

cards.question: How do you define an inline variable in a header file?
cards.code:
// constants.h
// Need to share this variable across multiple files
cards.explanation: The 'inline' keyword allows the variable to be defined in a header file and included in multiple translation units without violating ODR.

cards.front: What C++ version introduced inline variables?
Should you use the 'inline' keyword to try to optimize performance?
cards.back: No. Let the compiler decide. Modern compilers are better at determining what should be inlined. Use 'inline' primarily to allow definitions in header files.
Yes. Constexpr functions are implicitly inline, so they can be defined in header files without violating ODR.

cards.explanation:
In modern C++, the primary use of 'inline' is not for optimization but to:
  • Allow function definitions in header files
  • Allow variable definitions in header files (C++17)
  • Avoid violating the one-definition rule
The compiler will inline what it wants regardless of the keyword.
examples: inline int square(int x) { return x * x; }
// Can be included in multiple .cpp files safely

cards.text: To define a variable in a header file (C++17), use {{c1::inline}}: inline constexpr double pi{ 3.14159 };
Inline functions are exempt from the {{c1::one-definition rule}}, allowing them to be defined in {{c2::multiple translation units}}.
{{c1::Inline expansion}} replaces a function call with the code from the function body, eliminating {{c2::function call overhead}} but increasing {{c3::code size}}.
Constexpr functions are {{c1::implicitly inline}}, allowing them to be defined in {{c2::header files}}.
